missing: Quantified success metrics / target state. The doc is all qualitative ('feel amazing'). For a print-and-pin direction it should name 3-5 measurable north-star outcomes the work is graded on: e.g. time-to-clear-queue, approve-to-feedback latency target (<200ms perceived), AA-pass rate across interactive controls (currently failing), false-inbox-zero rate to 0, dark-mode-illegible-first-paint to 0. Without numbers, 'finish the last 30%' is unfalsifiable and the roadmap's 'outcome' lines are vibes.

The actual operator. There is no evidence the direction is grounded in real medspa-operator behavior (interviews, session recordings, support tickets, churn reasons). Every persona claim ('time-poor', 'clearing a morning queue', 'likely-default-dark audience', 'mobile-first') is asserted, not sourced. 'likely-default-dark' in particular is load-bearing for ranking dark mode CRITICAL yet is a guess. A design director would ask: what do we actually know about how these owners use it, on what device, at what hour?

Mobile/touch as a first-class surface. The doc is desktop-and-keyboard-forward (Superhuman j/k/a/e triage, sidebar nav, AgentPanel) despite repeatedly asserting the audience is 'mobile-first'. There is no touch-target sizing pass (44px), no thumb-reach/bottom-sheet ergonomics, no haptics spec, and the swipe physics are praised but not extended into a mobile interaction system. The keyboard-triage investment may be aimed at the wrong device for this audience.

Error/failure and empty states as a system. Beyond the 409 'already handled' case and skeletons, there's no treatment for: network-offline, API-down, partial-data, a halted/paused agent's screens, an agent that errored mid-action, or 'Alex is down' messaging. For a product that spends money and messages customers, the failure surface is under-specified relative to the happy path.

Notifications / out-of-app loop. The whole thesis is 'your employee handled things while you were away,' but there is no push/email/SMS spec for the one thing that needs you. A governed-autonomy cockpit lives or dies on its escalation channel; it's absent.

Verification/measurement plan for the craft claims. The doc asserts grain at 4%, spring bounce <=0.1, 150ms exits, etc., but never says how 'amazing' gets validated (no visual-regression, no perf budget tied to the perceived-performance section, no a11y CI gate proposed beyond the token drift-guard). It tells engineers what to build but not how anyone will know it landed.

Content/voice system beyond examples. The 'honest voice' is praised per-surface but there is no consolidated voice-and-tone spec or copy-token layer, so the same 'tell the truth' principle will be re-litigated per PR. Given how much of the trust thesis rides on copy, a small voice guide (number-spelling rule, attribution verbs, absence-phrasing) is a missing deliverable the doc gestures at but doesn't provide.
weakSpots: CONTRAST MATH IS PARTLY WRONG and a skeptic will catch it. Recomputed independently: white-on-amber at 30 55% 46% = 3.75:1 (doc says 3.76 - good, correctly fails AA). BUT the proposed fix 30 58% 38% = 5.13:1, not the doc's claimed '~4.65' - the fix overshoots and could retain more vibrancy. Worse: the deltaNeg claim that 'ink-3 ~3.6:1' is 'the lowest-contrast element' that 'fails' does NOT reproduce - #6B6052 on the #FAF8F2 canvas is 5.78:1, comfortably AA. The perceptual point ('bad news reads as low-emphasis') may still hold and it may sit on a tinted card, but the stated numbers and the 'fails AA' framing for the delta are not defensible as written. This is the doc's single most attackable evidence.

'Finish the last 30%' is asserted, never derived. It's a memorable frame and probably directionally true, but there is no accounting that yields 30% (vs 40% or 55%). Stating a precise number you can't show your work on invites the exact 'unsupported claim' critique the doc otherwise avoids. Either show the rough ledger (X of Y named gaps are wiring vs net-new) or soften to 'finishing, not redesigning.'

Effort/impact tags are unsubstantiated point-estimates. Most moves are 'High / Low'. Several tagged [quick win] are not: 'Rebuild Undo as a real safety net' touches the toast reducer, queueing, countdown binding, and styling (the doc itself tags it [systemic] in one table but the Wave-0 list implies same-week); 'wire decisions.feed() + onMutate optimistic removal' is correctly low-effort, but optimistic removal with rollback-on-error and reorder-on-poll is genuinely fiddly and under-scoped as 'Low'. The estimates read as confident guesses, which undercuts the otherwise-rigorous tone.

Token unification is called 'mechanical, not creative' - this oversells it. Migrating cockpit/tokens.ts hex -> hsl(var()) is mechanical for value substitution, but the doc simultaneously demands a NEW three-tier primitive/semantic/component architecture AND a full .dark palette for 178 tokens AND luminance-step decisions for agent hues - those ARE design decisions (which semantic names, which luminance steps, warm-near-black value). Calling the keystone 'mechanical' will make Wave 1's true cost surprise the team.

Potential tension between Principle VI ('Calm is not inert' / agents breathing, grain, motion) and the praised reduced-motion + OperatorCharacter-pauses-on-hidden-tab restraint. The doc mostly reconciles this (felt-not-seen, reduced-motion fallbacks), but 'an agent that breathes when it's working' mounted on Home + AgentPanel header + Inbox rows is a lot of always-on motion for a 'calm broadsheet'; the doc never sets a ceiling on how much simultaneous aliveness is too much, so it risks authorizing the noisy-dashboard outcome it warns against.

Roadmap dependency is mostly sound but has one inversion: Wave 2 'Switch the agents on' (avatar state from real status) and 'Rebuild Undo' don't strictly depend on Wave 1 token unification, yet several Wave-2 items (one canonical avatar color source) DO. The waves are described as cleanly sequential ('the keystone that makes everything after it enforceable') but a few Wave-2 wins are independent quick wins stranded behind a 1-2 week foundation wave. Worth calling out what in Wave 2 can run parallel to Wave 1.

Citation drift on a couple of CSS line anchors (e.g., this-week.module.css:96 for the drop-cap is slightly off the actual line; the feature is present). The doc pre-emptively flags ONE such anchor, which is honest, but it means not every ':NN' is a precise landing - a reviewer who spot-checks the wrong one loses confidence in all of them. Code/TS file:line claims, by contrast, all verified exact.

Inspiration board is excellent but slightly over-long and a few steals restate the same fix (Stripe/Wise/Ramp all -> the approval panel). Tighter would be stronger; as-is it risks reading as a curated-references flex rather than 12 distinct moves.
mustFix: Recompute and correct every contrast number, or remove the precise figures. Specifically: change the deltaNeg claim - #6B6052 on #FAF8F2 is 5.78:1 (passes AA), NOT '~3.6:1 / lowest-contrast / fails'. Reframe Gap #9 as a PERCEPTUAL-emphasis problem (bad news reads as low-priority) and verify what background the delta actually sits on before asserting a failure. Fix the proposed amber: 30 58% 38% = 5.13:1, not 4.65; pick a value that hits ~4.5 if you want to preserve vibrancy. Leave the white-on-amber 3.75:1 fail (that one is correct).

Add 3-5 quantified north-star metrics and bind each roadmap wave's 'Outcome' line to one. Without measurable targets, the entire 'make it feel amazing' deliverable is unfalsifiable and cannot be used to grade PRs as the doc claims ('every PR is measured against them').

Source or soften the load-bearing audience assumptions, especially 'likely-default-dark' and 'mobile-first.' The dark-mode CRITICAL ranking and the de-prioritization of touch ergonomics both hang on guesses. Either cite real telemetry/research or label them explicitly as hypotheses-to-validate so the severity ranking isn't built on assertion.

Right-size the effort tags. Re-tag 'Rebuild Undo' as [systemic] consistently (it appears as a Wave-0-adjacent quick fix in one place), scope 'onMutate optimistic removal' to include rollback-on-error + poll-reconcile (not just 'Low'), and add a one-line basis for any 'High/Low' that touches a reducer, queueing, or cross-surface state.

Stop calling token unification 'mechanical.' Split it honestly into (a) mechanical hex->hsl(var()) substitution [low risk] and (b) the genuinely creative new semantic-token architecture + full .dark luminance palette [design work], so Wave 1's true cost is visible and the team isn't surprised.

Add a mobile/touch interaction section (or explicitly defer it with a reason). Given the repeated 'mobile-first audience' claim, the absence of touch-target sizing, thumb-reach/bottom-sheet ergonomics, and haptics - while investing in keyboard j/k/a/e triage - is a coherence gap that contradicts the stated user.

Add an escalation/notification spec for the 'one thing that needs you.' A governed-autonomy cockpit whose whole pitch is 'handled while you were away' cannot omit how the operator is pulled back in (push/email/SMS). This is a missing surface, not a polish item.

Either show the rough ledger behind 'last 30%' or soften it to 'finishing, not redesigning.' Stating an unsupported precise percentage is the one place the doc violates its own anti-placeholder, tell-the-truth standard.
